title: OLAP Lakehouse: GCS + BigLake + Looker
description: GCP-native lakehouse pattern centered on customer-owned data in GCS, BigLake Iceberg tables in BigQuery for query and metadata management, and Looker for BI/reporting. Default posture uses BigLake-managed Iceberg tables to keep data in object storage while exposing SQL analytics and BI access through BigQuery-compatible interfaces. Best when teams want an open-format GCP lakehouse rather than a warehouse-first BigQuery-only serving posture.

cost.costNotes: GCS provides low-cost storage, BigLake/BigQuery query cost is consumption-based, and Looker adds BI/platform cost. This posture is attractive when open-format data in customer storage is a requirement.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min ($50): small GCS dataset, low-volume scans, and limited BI use. Max ($7,000): larger analytical scans and broader BI usage. Costs remain driven by scan volume and BI footprint rather than always-on warehouse capacity.
provenance.adoptionCost: Adoption cost covers GCS dataset layout, BigLake Iceberg table setup, metadata/governance wiring, and Looker model/report integration. Estimate 12-15 developer days at ~$160/day = $2,300.
provenance.licenseCost: No separate software license beyond GCP and BI service consumption.
provenance.source: LLM analysis informed by BigLake Iceberg table documentation (as of 13-Apr-2026)

provides.reasoning: BigLake Iceberg tables queried through BigQuery provide SQL analytics over GCS-resident lakehouse data.
Looker provides BI and semantic modeling on top of the BigQuery-accessible lakehouse data.
BigLake and BigQuery support batch and staged ingestion into GCS-backed analytical tables.
GCS is the primary analytical data plane and source of truth.
BigLake metadata and BigQuery table management provide cataloged access to the lakehouse datasets.
BigLake Iceberg tables provide an open-format lakehouse contract over customer-owned storage.

requires.reasoning: The lakehouse expects upstream ingestion and table maintenance into GCS-backed analytical datasets.
GCS, BigLake/BigQuery, and Looker all rely on cloud IAM and service identities.
The default posture assumes service-account-based access between ingestion, query, and BI layers on GCP.

availability.reason: General architectural pattern applicable to GCP-native lakehouse workloads

supports_nfr.reason: The default stack relies on BigLake/BigQuery over object storage and BI caching/modeling rather than warehouse-first cold-query serving.
The default stack spans GCS, metadata/query layers, and BI delivery. Three nines is a defensible default target.
Recovery depends on GCS data, BigLake/BigQuery metadata, and BI layers rather than a single always-on warehouse service.
Freshness is bounded by ingestion cadence and BigLake/Iceberg metadata update patterns.
The default stack targets moderate BI and analytical concurrency rather than the highest-concurrency warehouse serving posture.
The default lakehouse assumes staged or batch-oriented writes through GCS-backed table maintenance rather than high-frequency point updates.
BigLake/BigQuery lakehouse jobs depend on table metadata resolution and query-service startup overhead; the default GCP stack should not claim sub-15-second p95 starts.
Tail job starts are affected by metadata resolution, query planner cold paths, and orchestration latency in the GCP-native stack.
The default GCP lakehouse posture assumes moderate scheduled or event-driven batch activity rather than extremely high job churn.
The lakehouse is compatible with multiple application auth choices because GCP IAM governs the analytical control plane separately.
GCP provides auditability across storage, query, and metadata services.
PII can be handled through IAM, dataset controls, and governance practices.
GDPR compatibility depends on data handling and governance rather than the lakehouse stack alone.
HIPAA compatibility depends on encryption, IAM, audit logging, and organizational controls.
CCPA compatibility depends on data discovery and deletion workflows across the lakehouse.
SOX compatibility depends on change control, access control, and report/audit governance.
GCS-backed datasets can support strict durability when configured appropriately.

supports_constraints.reason: GCP lakehouse access is SQL- and API-driven and does not constrain the application language.
GCP lakehouse can serve the analytical plane behind web, mobile, desktop, and API products while also supporting worker, batch, and data-pipeline execution.
Primary gate: this variant is for OLAP analytical workloads.

storage_layer.description: Default analytical storage layer. GCS is the source of truth for the lakehouse.

query_engine.description: BigLake Iceberg through BigQuery is the default because it provides a managed open-format lakehouse posture on GCP.

metadata_catalog.description: BigLake is the default metadata/catalog surface. Add Dataplex when stronger data governance is required.

table_format.description: Iceberg is the default because this pattern is intentionally lakehouse-oriented and BigLake explicitly supports Iceberg tables.

bi_layer.description: Looker is the default semantic and BI layer for the GCP lakehouse posture.

ingestion_mode.description: Default ingestion writes curated analytical data to GCS-backed Iceberg tables. Streaming ingestion is supported through the Storage Write API and compatible connectors.
